title: M/s Infosys BPM Limited v. Rajesh Kumar Sharma
keyArguments: The respondent employee was terminated for cause following a duly conducted domestic inquiry in accordance with the principles of natural justice and the company's Standing Orders.
The charges of misconduct including unauthorized disclosure of confidential client data were substantiated by documentary evidence and witness testimonies during the inquiry proceedings.
The employee was afforded adequate opportunity to cross-examine witnesses and present his defence, thereby satisfying the requirements of a fair hearing under the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947.
The Labour Court erred in interfering with the findings of the Inquiry Officer without returning a finding of perversity in the domestic inquiry proceedings.
The quantum of punishment was commensurate with the gravity of the misconduct and consistent with past precedents within the organization.
judgmentSummary: The Delhi High Court set aside the Labour Court's award reinstating the respondent employee, holding that the domestic inquiry was conducted fairly and the findings of misconduct were supported by adequate evidence. The court found that the Labour Court had overstepped its jurisdiction by reappreciating evidence without any finding of perversity. The termination of the respondent by M/s Infosys BPM Limited was upheld as valid and in accordance with law.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
Writ Petition (Civil) No. 4471 of 2023

M/s Infosys BPM Limited ... Petitioner
versus
Rajesh Kumar Sharma & Anr. ... Respondents

Date of Decision: 14th September 2023

HON'BLE MR. JUSTICE SANDEEP KUMAR MATTA

JUDGMENT

1. The present writ petition has been filed under Articles 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India challenging the award dated 03rd January 2023 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court-VI, Karkardooma, Delhi, in ID Case No. 312 of 2019, whereby the Labour Court directed reinstatement of the respondent No. 1 workman with 50% back wages.

2. The brief facts of the case are that the respondent No. 1, Rajesh Kumar Sharma, was employed as a Senior Process Executive with the petitioner company since 12th March 2012. A charge sheet dated 17th April 2018 was issued to him alleging serious misconduct including unauthorized access and transmission of confidential client data to a third party in violation of the company's Information Security Policy and the terms of his employment contract.

3. A domestic inquiry was conducted by an independent Inquiry Officer wherein the respondent was given full opportunity to participate, cross-examine witnesses produced by the management, and lead his own evidence. The Inquiry Officer, after careful consideration of the material on record, returned a finding of proved misconduct vide report dated 21st July 2018. Thereafter, a show cause notice was issued to the respondent calling upon him to show cause why the punishment of dismissal should not be imposed. The respondent's reply was duly considered and an order of dismissal was passed on 10th September 2018.

4. The respondent raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the Labour Court. The Labour Court, vide the impugned award, held that the inquiry was vitiated on account of procedural irregularity and proceeded to hold on merits that the charges were not adequately proved.

5. This Court has carefully perused the record of the domestic inquiry proceedings. It is manifest from the record that the respondent was served with the charge sheet, was permitted to inspect documents, was represented by a co-worker of his choice, and was given full opportunity to cross-examine the management witnesses. The Inquiry Officer's report is detailed, reasoned, and based on documentary evidence including system access logs, email records, and deposition of the Information Security team.

6. It is well settled by the Supreme Court in Bharat Forge Co. Ltd. v. Uttam Manohar Nakate, (2005) 2 SCC 489, and reiterated in a long line of decisions, that a Labour Court or Industrial Tribunal exercising jurisdiction under Section 11-A of the Industrial Disputes Act, 1947, cannot interfere with the findings of a domestic inquiry merely by reappreciating evidence. Interference is warranted only when the findings are perverse, i.e., no reasonable person could have arrived at such a conclusion on the basis of the material available.

7. In the present case, the Labour Court has failed to return any finding of perversity. On the contrary, the Labour Court has substituted its own view for that of the Inquiry Officer on a pure question of appreciation of evidence. This approach is legally impermissible and amounts to an error of jurisdiction.

8. Furthermore, the allegation of procedural irregularity as found by the Labour Court is not borne out by the record. The inquiry proceedings were conducted in accordance with the certified Standing Orders of the establishment and in conformity with the principles of natural justice.

9. In view of the foregoing, this Court is of the considered opinion that the impugned award of the Labour Court is liable to be set aside. The termination of the respondent No. 1 is upheld as legal and valid. The writ petition is accordingly allowed. The impugned award dated 03rd January 2023 is quashed and set aside. No order as to costs.
